literal: root-nature, the primal unmanifest substrate; mūla 'root' + prakṛti 'nature, primordial matter'
invariant_definition: *Mūla-prakṛti* names the root or primal *prakṛti*: the unmanifest, undifferentiated equilibrium of *sattva*, *rajas*, and *tamas* prior to any evolute. In classical Sāṅkhya it is *avyakta-pradhāna*, the non-conscious causal substrate from which all manifest evolutes proceed and into which they return; it is itself uncaused, ubiquitous, and one. The term enters Vedānta through the *Brahma-Sūtra* polemic against Sāṅkhya, and is then reabsorbed differently by each school: identified with *māyā* in some Advaita formulations, treated as Bhagavān's *acit* energy in Viśiṣṭādvaita and Acintya-Bhedābheda, and explicitly retained as a real substance in Vijñānabhikṣu's late synthetic Sāṅkhya-Yoga-Vedānta. The dispute over *mūla-prakṛti* is therefore a dispute over whether there is a non-conscious eternal substrate alongside *brahman*/Bhagavān, and if so, what its dependence-relation is.

per_school.definition: *Mūla-prakṛti* is the unmanifest *pradhāna*, the eternal equilibrium of the three *guṇas* from which *mahat* and the rest of the twenty-three evolutes proceed. It is *avikṛta*, itself uncaused; it acts for the sake of *puruṣa*'s liberation; its productive activity ceases when *vivekakhyāti* is attained.

Advaita does not admit an eternal non-conscious substrate alongside Brahman. Where the term appears in Advaitic contexts it is read as *māyā* in its causal aspect — *avyākṛta* — qualifying Brahman and itself *anirvacanīya*. The *Brahma-Sūtra* refutation of Sāṅkhya at 2.2.1ff is read by Śaṅkara as foreclosing any reading on which *pradhāna* is the world's independent cause.

*Prakṛti* is real and eternal but always Bhagavān's *acit-vibhūti*, his body in the *śarīra-śarīrin* analysis. It is never an independent cause. Where *mūla-prakṛti* is invoked, it is the undifferentiated state of this acit-energy prior to cosmic differentiation; the analysis preserves Sāṅkhya's evolute scheme while subordinating *prakṛti* to the Lord.

*Mūla-prakṛti* is the undifferentiated state of Bhagavān's *bahiraṅgā-śakti* (*mahāmāyā*), eternally available to him and quiescent until the glance of *puruṣa-avatāras*. Jīva Gosvāmin systematizes this with the *Bhāgavata*'s account of *mahā-puruṣa* impregnating *prakṛti*.

Vijñānabhikṣu retains *mūla-prakṛti* as a real eternal substrate under the Lord. *Māyā* is identified with *prakṛti*, *māyin* with Maheśvara; the resulting picture is a *bhedābheda*-like theistic Sāṅkhya in which *prakṛti* is the ontological non-conscious principle and Bhagavān its presiding *Īśvara*.

translator_note: 'Primordial matter' is acceptable in Sāṅkhya contexts; 'root-nature' is closer to the etymology and is preferable when contrasting *mūla* (root) with *vikṛti* (evolute). Translating as 'Nature' (capitalized) is fine if the reader has been warned that it is non-conscious and pluralistically conceived.
